verses.text: Again Satan appears, and God commends Job, who still 'holdeth fast his integrity,' though moved against 'without cause.' The first trial only proved his faith. God glories in the steadfastness of His servant, and grief borne well is His delight.

Again Satan appears, and God commends Job, who still 'holdeth fast his integrity,' though moved against 'without cause.' The first trial only proved his faith. God glories in the steadfastness of His servant, and grief borne well is His delight.

Again Satan appears, and God commends Job, who still 'holdeth fast his integrity,' though moved against 'without cause.' The first trial only proved his faith. God glories in the steadfastness of His servant, and grief borne well is His delight.

Satan presses, 'skin for skin... touch his bone and his flesh, and he will curse thee'; God permits the affliction but spares his life. The accuser claims pain will break what loss could not. Still God sets the limit; the tempter cannot pass the line He draws.

Satan presses, 'skin for skin... touch his bone and his flesh, and he will curse thee'; God permits the affliction but spares his life. The accuser claims pain will break what loss could not. Still God sets the limit; the tempter cannot pass the line He draws.

Satan presses, 'skin for skin... touch his bone and his flesh, and he will curse thee'; God permits the affliction but spares his life. The accuser claims pain will break what loss could not. Still God sets the limit; the tempter cannot pass the line He draws.

Satan strikes Job with painful boils head to foot, and he sits among the ashes, scraping himself with a broken shard. Misery is heaped on misery. The man once honoured is brought to the dust, yet God has not let go of him.

Satan strikes Job with painful boils head to foot, and he sits among the ashes, scraping himself with a broken shard. Misery is heaped on misery. The man once honoured is brought to the dust, yet God has not let go of him.

His wife urges, 'curse God, and die,' but Job answers, 'shall we receive good at the hand of God, and shall we not receive evil?' and he does not sin with his lips. Even a beloved voice may counsel despair. Faith takes both sweet and bitter from the same loving hand.

His wife urges, 'curse God, and die,' but Job answers, 'shall we receive good at the hand of God, and shall we not receive evil?' and he does not sin with his lips. Even a beloved voice may counsel despair. Faith takes both sweet and bitter from the same loving hand.

Three friends come from afar to mourn with Job, weep aloud, and sit with him seven days in silence, his suffering too great for words. Their first kindness is wordless presence. Often the truest comfort to deep grief is simply to sit beside it and share the sorrow.
